title: What Is Dollar Cost Averaging? The Beginner's Guide
desc: Dollar cost averaging explained — what it is, why it works, and how to automate it for stocks, ETFs, and crypto.

sections.body:
Dollar cost averaging (DCA) means investing a fixed dollar amount on a regular schedule — weekly, biweekly, or monthly — regardless of what the market is doing.

When prices are low, your fixed amount buys more shares. When prices are high, it buys fewer. Over time, this smooths out your average purchase cost.

DCA works for two reasons:

**1. Removes emotion.** The biggest investing mistake is panic-selling during crashes. DCA forces you to keep buying even when everything looks scary — exactly when the best deals are available.

**2. Removes the need to time the market.** Even professional fund managers can't reliably time the market. DCA sidesteps the problem entirely.

Crypto DCA example: $100/week into Bitcoin starting January 2022 (near the peak). By 2025, that investor had a significant profit despite starting at what felt like the worst possible time.

Consistency beats timing. Use Coinbase to set up automatic weekly crypto buys and stop agonizing over price.

title: How to Open a Roth IRA in 2026 (Step-by-Step)
desc: A complete beginner's guide to Roth IRAs — what they are, why they're powerful, 2026 contribution limits, and how to open one in minutes.

sections.body:
IRA = Individual Retirement Account. The 'Roth' version has a unique tax structure: pay taxes now, never pay taxes again on the growth.

For most people under 40, the Roth IRA is the single best investment account available — better than a regular brokerage account because of tax-free compounding.

In 2026, you can contribute up to **$7,000/year** to a Roth IRA ($8,000 if you're 50+).

Income limits: full contribution allowed under $146,000 (single) or $230,000 (married filing jointly).

$7,000/year = ~$583/month = ~$134/week.

The barrier is lower than most people think:
• No minimum balance at most brokers
• 10–15 minutes to set up
• Start investing the same day

The most common mistake: waiting until you have 'enough.' Start with $50. Time is the main ingredient — and every year you wait costs you thousands in compounding.

sections.body:

Instead of picking individual stocks, you buy tiny pieces of all 500 companies at once. When the market goes up, your fund goes up.

The S&P 500 has returned an average of ~10.5% annually since 1957.

Professional fund managers — with MBAs, Bloomberg terminals, and decades of experience — can't beat a simple index fund 9 out of 10 times. Yet they charge 1–2% in annual fees.

title: Lightning Network Explained: Why Bitcoin Payments Are Now Instant
desc: The Bitcoin Lightning Network explained simply — what it is, how it makes Bitcoin instant and free, and why Strike is built on it.

sections.body:
Bitcoin is great as a store of value but has a limitation: the main blockchain processes only ~7 transactions per second. Visa handles 24,000 per second.

Bitcoin transactions can take 10+ minutes and cost $5–$50 in fees during busy periods — terrible for everyday payments. The Lightning Network solves this.

Lightning creates 'payment channels' between users — like running a tab at a bar, settling multiple transactions at once.

Inside a Lightning channel: transactions are **instant and nearly free**. Only opening and closing a channel touches the main blockchain.

Result: thousands of transactions per second, fees under $0.01, settlement in milliseconds.

Strike abstracts away all Lightning Network complexity into a clean, simple app. You don't need to understand payment channels — it just works.

For buying Bitcoin, Strike is one of the lowest-fee options available. The first $500 is completely free, and fees after that remain very competitive.

title: Debt Avalanche vs Snowball: Which Method Saves More Money?
desc: A clear comparison of the debt avalanche and snowball methods — which saves more money, which keeps you motivated, and exactly how to choose.

sections.body:
**Debt Avalanche:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the highest-interest debt first. Mathematically optimal — saves the most in interest.

**Debt Snowball:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the smallest balance first. Psychologically optimal — quick wins keep you motivated.

Mathematically, the avalanche wins every time. The gap is largest when you have high-interest debt (credit cards), a long timeline, and large rate differences between debts.

For most people with credit card debt, the avalanche saves $1,000–$3,000+ in interest.

The best debt payoff method is the one you actually stick with.

Research shows most people fail at debt payoff not because of a bad strategy but because they lose motivation. The snowball's quick wins combat this.

If you're disciplined: use the avalanche. If you need emotional momentum: use the snowball. Either beats minimum payments.

title: Emergency Fund 101: Build One Even on a Tight Budget
desc: How much to save in your emergency fund, where to keep it, and the fastest way to build one — even on a tight budget.

sections.h: How Much Do You Actually Need?
sections.body:
The classic advice is 3–6 months of expenses. But the right amount depends on your situation:

• **Stable job, dual income:** 3 months is enough
• **Single income or variable income:** 6 months minimum
• **Self-employed or freelance:** 9–12 months recommended

The goal: be able to pay your bills for that many months if all income stopped tomorrow.

Never keep your emergency fund in stocks or crypto. The whole point is that it's there when you need it — markets can be down 40% exactly when you lose your job.

Keep it in a HYSA: FDIC insured, earns 4–5% APY (vs 0.01% at big banks), and you can access it in 24 hours.

Break it into milestones:

**Month 1–2:** Save $500 — covers most minor emergencies
**Month 3–4:** Reach $1,000 — covers car repairs, medical bills
**Month 5+:** Continue until you hit 3 months of expenses

Treat your emergency fund contribution like a bill. Set up an automatic transfer on payday before you can spend the money.

title: Compound Interest Explained: The Math That Makes or Breaks Your Wealth
desc: Compound interest explained simply with real examples — how it builds wealth and why starting early is the most important financial decision you can make.

sections.body:
Compound interest means your money earns money on its money — and that money earns money on that money.

The formula: **A = P(1 + r)^t**

At 7%: your money grows 3.9× in 20 years, 7.6× in 30 years, 14.9× in 40 years. Time is the main variable.

Quick mental math: divide 72 by your interest rate to find years to double your money.

• At 6% → doubles every 12 years
• At 7% → doubles every ~10 years
• At 10% → doubles every 7.2 years

S&P 500 historical return (~10%): your money doubles roughly every 7 years.

sections.h: Why Starting Early Beats Earning More
sections.body:
Time is worth more than money in investing. Starting at 25 vs 35 isn't a 10-year head start — it's potentially hundreds of thousands of dollars at retirement.

Every year you delay is compounding you can never recover. The best time to start is right now.

title: Compound Interest Explained: The Math That Makes or Breaks Your Wealth
desc: Compound interest explained simply with real examples — how it builds wealth and why starting early is the most important financial decision you can make.

sections.body:
Simple interest earns returns on your original investment only. Compound interest earns returns on your investment **plus all previous returns**.

• $10,000 at 7% simple for 30 years → **$31,000**
• $10,000 at 7% compound for 30 years → **$76,122**

Same money, same rate, same time. The difference is whether returns earn returns.

Doubling times by rate:

title: How Dollar Cost Averaging Removes Emotion From Investing
desc: Dollar cost averaging explained — what it is, why it works, and how to automate it for stocks, ETFs, and crypto.

sections.body:
Dollar cost averaging (DCA) means investing a fixed dollar amount on a regular schedule — weekly, biweekly, or monthly — regardless of what the market is doing.

When prices are low, your fixed amount buys more shares. When prices are high, it buys fewer. Over time, this smooths out your average purchase cost.

DCA works for two reasons:

**1. Removes emotion.** The biggest investing mistake is panic-selling during crashes. DCA forces you to keep buying even when everything looks scary — exactly when the best deals are available.

**2. Removes the need to time the market.** Even professional fund managers can't reliably time the market. DCA sidesteps the problem entirely.

Crypto DCA example: $100/week into Bitcoin starting January 2022 (near the peak). By 2025, that investor had a significant profit despite starting at what felt like the worst possible time.

Consistency beats timing. Use Coinbase to set up automatic weekly crypto buys and stop agonizing over price.

title: Lightning Network Explained: Why Bitcoin Payments Are Now Instant
desc: The Bitcoin Lightning Network explained simply — what it is, how it makes Bitcoin instant and free, and why Strike is built on it.

sections.body:
Bitcoin's blockchain is intentionally slow and secure. Every transaction is permanently recorded on a distributed ledger verified by thousands of nodes worldwide. This security comes with a cost: speed and fees.

For buying coffee or sending $5, waiting 10 minutes and paying $20 doesn't work. Lightning is Bitcoin's answer.

Lightning Network is a 'second layer' on top of Bitcoin:

1. Two parties open a payment channel (one blockchain transaction)
2. Exchange unlimited instant payments through that channel
3. Close the channel when done (one more blockchain transaction)

Net result: instant Bitcoin payments at essentially zero cost.

Strike abstracts away all Lightning Network complexity into a clean, simple app. You don't need to understand payment channels — it just works.

For buying Bitcoin, Strike is one of the lowest-fee options available. The first $500 is completely free, and fees after that remain very competitive.

Never keep your emergency fund in stocks or crypto. The whole point is that it's there when you need it — markets can be down 40% exactly when you lose your job.

title: What Is the Bitcoin Lightning Network? A Simple Explanation

sections.body:
Bitcoin's blockchain is intentionally slow and secure. Every transaction is permanently recorded on a distributed ledger verified by thousands of nodes worldwide. This security comes with a cost: speed and fees.

For buying coffee or sending $5, waiting 10 minutes and paying $20 doesn't work. Lightning is Bitcoin's answer.

Lightning creates 'payment channels' between users — like running a tab at a bar, settling multiple transactions at once.

Inside a Lightning channel: transactions are **instant and nearly free**. Only opening and closing a channel touches the main blockchain.

Result: thousands of transactions per second, fees under $0.01, settlement in milliseconds.

title: Debt Avalanche vs Snowball: Which Method Saves More Money?
desc: A clear comparison of the debt avalanche and snowball methods — which saves more money, which keeps you motivated, and exactly how to choose.

**Debt Avalanche:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the highest-interest debt first. Mathematically optimal — saves the most in interest.

**Debt Snowball:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the smallest balance first. Psychologically optimal — quick wins keep you motivated.

Mathematically, the avalanche wins every time. The gap is largest when you have high-interest debt (credit cards), a long timeline, and large rate differences between debts.

For most people with credit card debt, the avalanche saves $1,000–$3,000+ in interest.

The best debt payoff method is the one you actually stick with.

Research shows most people fail at debt payoff not because of a bad strategy but because they lose motivation. The snowball's quick wins combat this.

If you're disciplined: use the avalanche. If you need emotional momentum: use the snowball. Either beats minimum payments.

title: What Is the Bitcoin Lightning Network? A Simple Explanation
desc: The Bitcoin Lightning Network explained simply — what it is, how it makes Bitcoin instant and free, and why Strike is built on it.

sections.body:
Bitcoin's blockchain is intentionally slow and secure. Every transaction is permanently recorded on a distributed ledger verified by thousands of nodes worldwide. This security comes with a cost: speed and fees.

For buying coffee or sending $5, waiting 10 minutes and paying $20 doesn't work. Lightning is Bitcoin's answer.

Lightning Network is a 'second layer' on top of Bitcoin:

1. Two parties open a payment channel (one blockchain transaction)
2. Exchange unlimited instant payments through that channel
3. Close the channel when done (one more blockchain transaction)

Net result: instant Bitcoin payments at essentially zero cost.

Strike abstracts away all Lightning Network complexity into a clean, simple app. You don't need to understand payment channels — it just works.

For buying Bitcoin, Strike is one of the lowest-fee options available. The first $500 is completely free, and fees after that remain very competitive.

title: Dollar Cost Averaging: The Strategy That Works While You Sleep
desc: Dollar cost averaging explained — what it is, why it works, and how to automate it for stocks, ETFs, and crypto.

sections.body:
Dollar cost averaging (DCA) means investing a fixed dollar amount on a regular schedule — weekly, biweekly, or monthly — regardless of what the market is doing.

When prices are low, your fixed amount buys more shares. When prices are high, it buys fewer. Over time, this smooths out your average purchase cost.

DCA works for two reasons:

**1. Removes emotion.** The biggest investing mistake is panic-selling during crashes. DCA forces you to keep buying even when everything looks scary — exactly when the best deals are available.

**2. Removes the need to time the market.** Even professional fund managers can't reliably time the market. DCA sidesteps the problem entirely.

Crypto DCA example: $100/week into Bitcoin starting January 2022 (near the peak). By 2025, that investor had a significant profit despite starting at what felt like the worst possible time.

Consistency beats timing. Use Coinbase to set up automatic weekly crypto buys and stop agonizing over price.

title: How Compound Interest Works: Why Einstein Called It the 8th Wonder
desc: Compound interest explained simply with real examples — how it builds wealth and why starting early is the most important financial decision you can make.

sections.body:
Simple interest earns returns on your original investment only. Compound interest earns returns on your investment **plus all previous returns**.

• $10,000 at 7% simple for 30 years → **$31,000**
• $10,000 at 7% compound for 30 years → **$76,122**

Same money, same rate, same time. The difference is whether returns earn returns.

Quick mental math: divide 72 by your interest rate to find years to double your money.

• At 6% → doubles every 12 years
• At 7% → doubles every ~10 years
• At 10% → doubles every 7.2 years

S&P 500 historical return (~10%): your money doubles roughly every 7 years.

**Alex** invests $5,000/year from age 25–35 (10 years), then stops. Total invested: $50,000.
**Jordan** invests $5,000/year from age 35–65 (30 years). Total invested: $150,000.

At 65 (7% return):
• Alex: **$602,000**
• Jordan: **$472,000**

Alex invested a third as much money and still won by $130,000. Starting a decade earlier overwhelms 30 extra years of contributions.

title: How to Pay Off Debt Fast: Avalanche vs Snowball Explained
desc: A clear comparison of the debt avalanche and snowball methods — which saves more money, which keeps you motivated, and exactly how to choose.

sections.body:
Both methods are identical except for which debt gets your extra payment each month.

• **Avalanche** = highest interest rate first
• **Snowball** = smallest balance first

Both dramatically accelerate payoff compared to minimum payments only.

The avalanche always saves more interest — sometimes significantly.

Example: $20,000 across three debts (15% credit card, 8% personal loan, 4% car loan), $300 extra/month:

• **Avalanche:** Debt-free in 48 months, $4,200 in interest
• **Snowball:** Debt-free in 51 months, $5,100 in interest

$900 more in your pocket with avalanche.

The best debt payoff method is the one you actually stick with.

Research shows most people fail at debt payoff not because of a bad strategy but because they lose motivation. The snowball's quick wins combat this.

If you're disciplined: use the avalanche. If you need emotional momentum: use the snowball. Either beats minimum payments.

title: Emergency Fund 101: Build One Even on a Tight Budget
desc: How much to save in your emergency fund, where to keep it, and the fastest way to build one — even on a tight budget.

sections.h: How Much Do You Actually Need?
sections.body:
Define 'expenses' correctly: rent/mortgage, food, utilities, insurance, and minimum debt payments. Not your lifestyle spending.

Most people overestimate their required emergency fund. Start with 1 month — it covers most real emergencies — then build toward 3.

Your emergency fund should be:
1. **Liquid** — accessible in 24 hours
2. **Safe** — FDIC insured
3. **Earning interest** — not sitting in a 0.01% APY checking account

The best home is a **high-yield savings account (HYSA)**. Top HYSAs pay 4–5% APY — on a $10,000 fund, that's $400–$500/year in free interest.

Break it into milestones:

**Month 1–2:** Save $500 — covers most minor emergencies
**Month 3–4:** Reach $1,000 — covers car repairs, medical bills
**Month 5+:** Continue until you hit 3 months of expenses

Treat your emergency fund contribution like a bill. Set up an automatic transfer on payday before you can spend the money.

title: Strike and Lightning Network: How Bitcoin Became Usable Every Day
desc: The Bitcoin Lightning Network explained simply — what it is, how it makes Bitcoin instant and free, and why Strike is built on it.

sections.body:
Bitcoin is great as a store of value but has a limitation: the main blockchain processes only ~7 transactions per second. Visa handles 24,000 per second.

Bitcoin transactions can take 10+ minutes and cost $5–$50 in fees during busy periods — terrible for everyday payments. The Lightning Network solves this.

Lightning creates 'payment channels' between users — like running a tab at a bar, settling multiple transactions at once.

Inside a Lightning channel: transactions are **instant and nearly free**. Only opening and closing a channel touches the main blockchain.

Result: thousands of transactions per second, fees under $0.01, settlement in milliseconds.

Strike abstracts away all Lightning Network complexity into a clean, simple app. You don't need to understand payment channels — it just works.

For buying Bitcoin, Strike is one of the lowest-fee options available. The first $500 is completely free, and fees after that remain very competitive.

title: The Best Way to Pay Off Multiple Debts in 2026
desc: A clear comparison of the debt avalanche and snowball methods — which saves more money, which keeps you motivated, and exactly how to choose.

sections.body:
**Debt Avalanche:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the highest-interest debt first. Mathematically optimal — saves the most in interest.

**Debt Snowball:** Pay minimums on all debts, throw extra money at the smallest balance first. Psychologically optimal — quick wins keep you motivated.

Mathematically, the avalanche wins every time. The gap is largest when you have high-interest debt (credit cards), a long timeline, and large rate differences between debts.

For most people with credit card debt, the avalanche saves $1,000–$3,000+ in interest.

Dave Ramsey advocates the snowball because he's seen thousands fail with mathematically optimal plans they couldn't sustain.

But if you can stay motivated with the avalanche, the extra savings are real money. Use our free debt payoff calculator to see the exact numbers for your situation.

title: The Power of Compound Interest: Why Starting Early Changes Everything
desc: Compound interest explained simply with real examples — how it builds wealth and why starting early is the most important financial decision you can make.

sections.body:
Compound interest means your money earns money on its money — and that money earns money on that money.

The formula: **A = P(1 + r)^t**

At 7%: your money grows 3.9× in 20 years, 7.6× in 30 years, 14.9× in 40 years. Time is the main variable.

Doubling times by rate:

| Rate | Years to Double |
|------|----------------|
| 4% | 18 years |
| 6% | 12 years |
| 8% | 9 years |
| 10% | 7.2 years |

This is why starting early matters more than starting with a large amount.

sections.h: Why Starting Early Beats Earning More
sections.body:
**Alex** invests $5,000/year from age 25–35 (10 years), then stops. Total invested: $50,000.
**Jordan** invests $5,000/year from age 35–65 (30 years). Total invested: $150,000.

At 65 (7% return):
• Alex: **$602,000**
• Jordan: **$472,000**

Alex invested a third as much money and still won by $130,000. Starting a decade earlier overwhelms 30 extra years of contributions.
